paper_title: OmniLottie: Generating Vector Animations via Parameterized Lottie Tokens

sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: OmniLottie is a versatile framework that generates high quality vector animations from multi-modal instructions. For flexible motion and visual content control, we focus on Lottie, a light weight JSON formatting for both shapes and animation behaviors representation. However, the raw Lottie JSON files contain extensive invariant structural metadata and formatting tokens, posing significant challenges for learning vector animation generation. Therefore, we introduce a well designed Lottie tokenizer that transforms JSON files into structured sequences of commands and parameters representing shapes, animation functions and control parameters. Such tokenizer enables us to build OmniLottie upon pretrained vision language models to follow multi-modal interleaved instructions and generate high quality vector animations. To further advance research in vector animation generation, we curate MMLottie-2M, a large scale dataset of professionally designed vector animations paired with textual and visual annotations. With extensive experiments, we validate that OmniLottie can produce vivid and semantically aligned vector animations that adhere closely to multi modal human instructions.

sections.content: Figure 1. OmniLottie is Versatile Auto-regressive Generative Model for High Quality Lottie Animations. With user inputs of interleaved multi-modal instructions, OmniLottie supports tasks including text-to-Lottie, text-image-to-Lottie and video-to-Lottie generation. This broad capability makes it powerful and flexible solution for wide range of creative and design-oriented tasks.

sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: OmniLottie is versatile framework that generates high-quality vector animations from multi-modal instructions. For flexible motion and visual content control, we focus on Lottie, light-weight JSON formatting for both shapes and animation behaviors representation. However, the raw Lottie JSON files contain extensive invariant structural metadata and formatting tokens, posing significant challenges for learning vector animation generation. Therefore, we introduce well-designed Lottie tokenizer that transforms JSON files into structured sequences of commands and parameters representing shapes, animation functions and control parameters. Such tokenizer enables us to build OmniLottie upon pretrained visionlanguage models to follow multi-modal interleaved Project lead. Corresponding authors. instructions and generate high-quality vector animations. To further advance research in vector animation generation, we curate MMLottie-2M, large-scale dataset of professionally designed vector animations paired with textual and visual annotations. With extensive experiments, we validate that OmniLottie can produce vivid and semantically aligned vector animations that adhere closely to multi-modal human instructions. 1. Introduction Vector animation is type of computer animation created using vector graphics rather than raster videos [14, 55, 56]. Compared to raster videos, vector animations provide lightweight, editing-friendly, and resolutionindependent representation that facilitates intuitive manipulation of appearance, stylistic effects, and motion attributes. Therefore, vector animation is widely adopted across modern design workflows. Developing autonomous systems capable of generating vector animations from multi-modal instructions has the potential to significantly lower the entry barrier for creation and greatly accelerate professional design pipelines. Prior approaches for vector animation generation mainly focus on applying motion priors derived from either reference GIF [56] or text-to-video generator [14, 36, 48, 53, 55] to manually designed static vector graphics. While considering larger scope of computer animations, the diffusion-based video generation models [20, 23, 34, 47, 65, 66] have also demonstrated remarkably capability in generating rasterized image animation from texts and images. Though the above mentioned models are able to generate visually striking animations, their outputs inherently lack editability, cross-platform compatibility, and resolution scalability provided by vector formats. In this work, we focus on the Lottie representation, widely adopted vector animation format that stores all the shape, effect, and motion parameters within single JSON file. Lottie is complex hierarchical representation with layers and inheritance to integrate visual elements and motion effects. Compared to another widely adopted vector graphics representation, i.e. SVG stacks rects, circles, and paths for shapes and embeds CSS codes for animation. The superior cross-platform compatibility of Lottie makes it more popular than SVG. With the rapid advancement of Large Language Models (LLMs), we notice some existing models [3, 37, 42] are already able to generate Lottie animations through producing JSON text files, but suffer from unsatisfactory generation success rates (Tab. 1) because of the strict formatting of Lotties nested JSON representations and instruction-following performance (Figs. 4 to 6). We argue that directly generating raw Lottie JSONs is inefficient: formatting tokens and metadata take up large portions of the tokens rather than meaningful geometric or motion cues. In order to improve the robustness, efficiency and reliability of vector animation generation, we propose Lottie tokenizer that converts Lottie files into compact sequences of shape, effect, and animation commands with their associated parameters. The proposed serialization of Lottie tokens enables us to train auto-regressive models end-to-end on large scale datasets using the best practices from VLM training. To this end, we construct MMLottie-2M, comprehensive dataset of vector animations annotated with text descriptions, keyframe images, and rendered videos, to facilitate researches on Text-to-, Text-Image-to-, and Video-to-Lottie generation under unified framework. Building on MMLottie-2M, we further establish standardized evaluation protocols tailored to multi-modal Lottie generation. Our experiments demonstrate that OmniLottie significantly outperforms strong baselines in both visual fidelity and semantic alignment with multimodal inputs, delivering high-quality vector animations across diverse prompts and modalities. In summary, our contributions are as follows: We introduce OmniLottie, the first end-to-end framework capable of generating vector animations directly from multi-modal instructions. We curate MMLottie-2M, large-scale multi-modal dataset of two million Lottie animations with paired text descriptions, reference images, and video demonstrations, which has been publicly released to facilitate future research. We propose novel Lottie tokenizer that converts raw JSON into concise command sequences, improving both training efficiency and generation quality. Extensive evaluations show that OmniLottie produces high-quality vector animations and achieves SOTA performance in both visual fidelity and semantic alignment. 2. Related Work Vector Animation offers lightweight, resolutionindependent representation that encodes appearance and temporal dynamics via structured shapes, effects, and control parameters. Early SVG-based methods relied on simple interpolation [5, 58], which limits the motion expressiveness. Later approaches incorporated external motion priors from reference GIFs [56] or Text-to-video models [14, 45, 55, 63], and commercial tools such as LottieFiles Motion Copilot automate keyframe generation to ease manual workflows. However, these techniques decouple appearance and motion, relying on predefined dynamics or applying effects only to selected elements, preventing fully vectorized end-to-end animation synthesis. We instead adopt the Lottie format, which unifies shapes, effects, and animations within single JSON representation. Its simplicity allows modern VLMs to leverage strong instruction-following and multimodal reasoning, enabling OmniLottie to learn end-to-end vector animation generation across diverse multimodal settings. Vector Graphics are widely adopted in UI/UX, branding, and digital publishing. Prior work has proposed RNN-, VAE-, and transformer-based models [4, 5, 17, 19, 27, 31, 35, 38, 41, 44, 54, 58] as well as diffusion-based approaches [39, 58, 67] for SVG synthesis. With the rise of LLMs/VLMs [2, 3, 37, 42], autoregressive SVG generators [41, 57, 60] have demonstrated strong geometric and stylistic fidelity. Unlike static vector graphics, vector animation additionally models temporal and effect Figure 2. Overview of the Vector Animation Data Construction Pipeline. Data Collection & Conversion : We convert SVG assets into static Lottie files and apply randomized animation effects to generate effect-tagged animated Lotties. In parallel, we gather professionally created Lottie animations from five online platforms and perform thorough filtering and cleaning. Data Processing : Each animation undergoes spatio-temporal normalization, followed by video rendering and random keyframe extraction. Data Annotation : Finally, we provide multi-granularity annotations emphasizing geometric structure, color attributes, and motion characteristics. dynamics. OmniLottie advances this line by jointly synthesizing appearance, effects, and motion in unified autoregressive framework. Visual Autoregressive Generation. Autoregressive models treat visual content as token sequences and have been applied to images [24, 32, 40, 43], videos [8, 50, 52, 62], vector graphics [12, 33, 57, 60], and 3D [7, 9, 11, 22, 61, 64]. Recent work replaces discrete heads with diffusion-based continuous predictors [13, 25], and integrates pretrained VLMs for multimodal understanding [10, 21, 28, 49, 51]. We extend this paradigm to vector animation. By converting Lottie JSON into compact commandparameter sequences, we make complex animations amenable to sequence modeling. Built on pretrained VLM [3], OmniLottie processes multimodal instructions and autoregressively generates Lottie commands, enabling controllable, high-fidelity vector animation synthesis. 3. The MMLottie-2M Dataset To support training and evaluation of multi-modal vector animation generation, we curate the first large-scale Lottie dataset, MMLottie-2M. In this section, we detail our data curation and processing pipeline, as well as standardized evaluation protocol, MMLottie-Bench for the multi-modal generation tasks. 3.1. MMLottie-2M Lottie Data Curation. We construct the MMLottie-2M dataset by collecting Lottie animations from several major online platforms, including LottieFiles , IconScout , Flaticon , Iconfont , and Icons8. Web-crawled Lottie files often include irrelevant elements, such as base64 image layers, non-visual layers (audio, camera), and After Effects expressions that dynamically modify properties. These components complicate parameterization and are non-essential for rendering. We therefore remove such elements and discard files containing non-parameterizable layers to ensure clean, fully parameterizable data. SVG-Lottie Data Curation. Direct learning from Lottie files is challenging due to their complex JSON structure, intricate layer indexing, and entangled visual and motion semantics. To simplify training, we generate an auxiliary Lottie dataset by animating static SVGs with predefined motions (e.g., translations, zooms, rotations), based on the large-scale OmniSVG [60] collection. This decouples content from motion, improving alignment between visual components and animation conditions. Data Normalization. To standardize training and evaluation procedures, all Lottie JSON files are normalized spatially to 512 512 canvas and temporally to 016 timestamp range, with center alignment preserving aspect ratio for non-square animations. Motion Library for Data Augmentation. To mitigate the scarcity of native Lottie animations and close the distribution gap between static SVG conversions and authentic motion graphics, we develop motion transfer pipeline. We analyze 1M native Lottie files to extract transform trajectories (rotation, scale, position, opacity) across keyframes, generating motion signatures that encode temporal patterns (e.g., fade-in + upward motion + scale-down). By clustering semantically similar patterns with their caption keywords, we extract numerous canonical motion templates. These templates are then applied to static SVG-derived Lottie files through autoFigure 3. Overview of OmniLottie. Lottie Structure : We reorganize the Lottie JSON representation, with particular focus on the structure of its layers, including both common layer attributes and five special layer types. Lottie Parametrization : The hierarchical JSON format of Lottie is flattened into sequence of function calls, which are further parameterized to define dedicated vocabulary and token set for Lottie. OmniLottie : Built upon this parameterization, OmniLottie extends Qwen2.5-VL with new tokenizer and vocabulary for Lottie, and is trained on our curated Lottie dataset. mated keyframe injection, yielding massive scale of potential training instances. This approach drastically minimizes the path distribution gap while significantly broadening animated layer coverage, substantially improving the models exposure to diverse motion dynamics without requiring additional manual animation efforts. Lottie Data Annotation. To support practical vector animation generation, we focus on three tasks: text-to-, image-text-to-, and videoto-Lottie. Lottie files are rendered into videos with random light-colored backgrounds for VLM-based annotation. Due to frame limitations, we adopt coarse-to-fine strategy, the VLM first generates brief overall caption describing subjects, objects, motion, color, and style, then provides finer temporal details across frames using cues like begins with and then. Keywords emphasizing geometry and motion are highlighted to improve textfollowing accuracy  (Fig. 2)  . For Text-Image-to-Lottie, single frame is randomly selected from the rendered video, and the VLM is prompted to focus on foreground object motions. For Video-to-Lottie, the rendered videos directly serve as multi-modal instructions, simplifying annotation. 3.2. MMLottie-Bench The lack of mature and standardized benchmarks and metrics for vector animation generation poses significant challenges in evaluating (1) the quality of generated vector animations and (2) the extent to which generators faithfully follow multi-modal instructions. To address these issues, we introduce MMLottie-Bench, comprehensive evaluation protocol for multi-modal vector animation generation. Our goal is to construct benchmark that (1) reliably reflects models practical utility in real-world scenarios and (2) avoids the traintest overlap that commonly arises in conventional dataset splits. To this end, we construct an evaluation set, termed the Real Subset, consisting of 450 samples curated from artist-designed Lottie animations collected from professional designers. All evaluation samples are strictly disjoint from the training data, ensuring assessment on genuinely unseen, real-world content. Specifically, for the Text-to-Lottie task, we include 150 textual prompts derived from real Lottie animations. For the Text-Imageto-Lottie task, we extract one rendered frame together with its corresponding textual description from each of 150 real Lottie samples. For the Video-to-Lottie task, we include 150 rendered animation videos. In addition, to further ensure the fairness and longterm robustness of our benchmark, particularly to mitigate potential contamination from future models trained on overly similar data, we construct complementary Synthetic Subset via instruction-based synthesis using state-of-the-art generative models. Concretely, for the Text-to-Lottie task, we synthesize 150 textual prompts for each Lottie subset using GPT-4o. For the Text-Image-toLottie task, we synthesize 150 vector-style images using Gemini3.1-Pro Image, along with corresponding motion descriptions for evaluation. For the Video-to-Lottie task, we generate 150 additional shape descriptions paired with motion descriptions, which are then fed into Seedance 1.0 to produce reference videos. To enhance the transparency of the benchmark, we record and release the entire data synthesis process for the Synthetic Subset. MMLottie-Bench evaluates models along two key dimensions: visual quality and semantic alignment with the provided multi-modal inputs. 4. Proposed Method: OmniLottie OmniLottie consists of well-designed Lottie tokenizer that encodes/decodes Lottie JSON files to/from compact sequences of discrete tokens, and well-trained VLM [3] that can process images, videos, and texts as interleaved multi-modal instructions for auto-regressive Lottie generation. The generated Lottie tokens are then detokenized to Lottie JSONs for vector animations as illustrated in Fig. 3. 4.1. Preliminary: Lottie Layer Properties Lottie layer attributes can be grouped into three categories: Base layer properties capture fundamental metadata, including identifiers (nm, mn), layer type ty, indexing and hierarchy (ind, parent), and flags such as ddd (3D) and hd (hidden). Timing attributes (sr, ip, op, st) define each layers placement along the animation timeline. Visual layer properties govern appearance and rendering behavior, including geometric transforms (ks), orientation (ao), matte relations (tt, tp, td), masks (hasMask, masksProperties), effects (ef ), styles (sy), blend mode (bm), and rendering tags (cl, ln). The ct flag controls transformation collapsing. Specific layer properties depend on layer type: shapes for shape layers; refId for linking precomp, image, audio, and data assets; dimensions (w, h) for precomp clipping or solid-layer sizing; tm for time remapping; for text content; and solid-layer parameters (sw, sh, sc) defining width, height, and color. 4.2. OmniLottie Lottie Structure. Contemporary VLMs [3, 37, 42] can generate JSON directly, but producing full Lottie JSON is inefficient for vector animation synthesis. The format contains extensive, largely invariant structural metadata, causing models to waste capacity on reproducing formatting tokens instead of learning animation-relevant priors. This redundancy increases sequence length and distracts the model from semantically important elements such as shapes, effects, and temporal dynamics. To mitigate this, we reorganize the Lottie representation into set of core properties (v, fr, ip, op, w, h, nm, ddd, layers) and conditional properties (assets, markers, fonts, chars), which appear only when required by specific layer types. Absent conditional fields are assigned Algorithm 1: Lottie Tokenizer Input: Lottie JSON ; Type-specific scales {st}, offsets {ot}; Pretrained tokenizer Vtext Output: Token sequence (encoding) / Command sequence (decoding) // Encoding

Lottie Tokenizer abstracts Lottie animations into compact sequences of animation commands and control parameters, removing redundant metadata while preserving full generative flexibility. The tokenizer supports five fundamental layer types, each identified by unique type parameter: Precomposition (ty=0), Solid (ty=1), Null (ty=3), Shape (ty=4), and Text (ty=5). Each layer type requires specialized parsing strategies to maintain structural integrity and temporal accuracy. Based on the distinct attributes of each layer type, Lottie tokenizer encapsulates the input JSON into fundamental properties (including v, ip, op, h, w, nm, ddd) and five categorized Python objects corresponding to different layer attribute classifications. We formalize Lottie animation as structured hierarchy: = {M, L1, L2, ..., LN }, (1) where = {v, r, ip, op, w, h, nm, ddd} represents base metadata, and Li denotes individual layers. Each layer is parameterized by its type τ {0, 1, 3, 4, 5} and attributes information Aτ : Li = (τi, Aτi, Ti, Ei), (2) where Ti and Ei represent transformations and effects respectively. Thereby, Lottie tokenizer reconstructs the complete scene hierarchy through parent-child relationships, yielding tree-structured representation that provides lossless compression of the JSON format. This parameterized representation serves as the foundation for generating compact, semantically rich token sequence. The conversion systematically traverses each Lottie layer, serializing common attributes followed by typespecific properties into sequential tokens. By ignoring low-level JSON formatting, the model focuses on essential generative content. The Lottie Tokenizer formalizes this process (Algorithm 1), parsing metadata first, then processing each layer with its type and attributes. As shown in Fig. 3, fundamental attributes are serialized as animation v="...", and null layers are similarly encoded, yielding structured, hierarchical text representation suitable for efficient VLM processing. To adapt pretrained VLMs for Lottie generation, we map Lotties hierarchical structure into unified discrete vocabulary using an offset-based scheme. Distinct ranges are assigned to parameter types (temporal, spatial, transformations, and styles), avoiding token conflicts while preserving semantic coherence. Our offset-based tokenization maps continuous Lottie parameters to discrete tokens: token(x, t) = st + ot, (3) where is the parameter value, is the parameter type, st is the type-specific scale factor, and ot is the vocabulary offset. The complete discrete token representation is: = [CMD1, p1,1, ..., p1,k1, CMD2, ..., CMDM , pM,kM ]. (4) Model architecture. Building upon the token sequences generated through the offset-based tokenization scheme, we train our model to learn the underlying distribution of Lottie animations represented in this discrete vocabulary space. To process interleaved multimodal instructions, we adopt pre-trained VLM, specifically Qwen2.5-VL [3], as the backbone for OmniLottie. We introduce an additional set of randomly initialized Lottie vocabulary embeddings to the model to represent the command and parameter structures produced by the Lottie tokenizer. Since the Qwen2.5-VL backbone is extensively pre-trained on diverse VLM tasks for concise response generation conditioned on the interleaved video, image, and text instructions, the pre-trained weights provide an effective initialization for generating Lotties adhering to multi-modal instructions. Training Objective. Similar to training LLMs, we train OmniLottie to generate new Lottie tokens x[i] conditioned on all previous tokens x[<i] , as well as the provided multi-modal instructions xc, with the standard cross-entropy loss. θ = arg min θ (cid:88) i=1 (cid:16) log xc; x[<i] x[i] ; θ (cid:17) (5) 5. Experiments In this section, we first introduce the baselines and evaluation metrics (Sec. 5.1). We then present quantitative and qualitative evaluations in Sec. 5.2 and Sec. 5.3, respectively. We conduct ablation studies in Sec. 5.4 to analyze the contribution of each component in OmniLottie. 5.1. Baselines and Evaluation Metrics Baselines. For Text-to-Lottie, we compare OmniLottie with DeepSeek [26], Qwen2.5-VL [3], GPT-5 [37], and the commercial tool Recraft. For Text-Image-toLottie, we benchmark against Qwen2.5-VL, GPT-5, AniClipart [55], and Livesketch [15]. For Video-to-Lottie, we evaluate against Qwen2.5-VL, Gemini3.1-Pro [16], and GPT-5. All baselines use official implementations with recommended hyperparameters and preprocessing/- postprocessing. Evaluation Metrics. For Text-to-Lottie and Text-Imageto-Lottie, we report FVD [46] for visual quality and CLIP similarity [30] between prompts and rendered animation frames. We further introduce two caption-consistency metrics: Object Alignment (Obj. Align) (010), measuring object presence, type, count, visual traits, and spatial relations; and Motion Alignment (Motion Align) (010), assessing correctness of motion type, direction, magnitude, target objects, and smoothness, independent of object accuracy. Both metrics use Claude-3.5Sonnet [1] as an LLM judge; invalid generations are omitted, and blank outputs receive 0. For Video-to-Lottie, we report FVD, PSNR [18], SSIM [18], and DINO [29]. Model efficiency is evaluated by (1) token efficiency average token length of generated Lottie JSON using the Qwen2.5-VL tokenizer and (2) computational cost average generation time per sample, with closed-source timings including the full API latency for realistic comparison. Quantitative Evaluation We report quantitative results for all methods in Tab. 1 across Text-to-Lottie, Text-Image-to-Lottie, and Videoto-Lottie tasks. OmniLottie delivers consistently superior performance on all metrics. In Text-to-Lottie generation, it achieves near-perfect success rates, the best FVD, and the strongest motion alignment. Although commercial tools like Recraft obtain competitive object alignment and CLIP scores, they lag behind in token efficiency and motion fidelity, while open-source language models show very low or zero success rates. For Text-Image-to-Lottie, OmniLottie again ranks first in FVD, object alignment, and motion alignment, while maintaining high reliability. Methods such as AniClipart and Livesketch, despite reasonable CLIP scores when successful, exhibit low success rates and significantly longer runtimes, limiting their practical utility. In Video-to-Lottie, OmniLottie preserves temporal and structural fidelity most effectively, achieving the best FVD, PSNR, SSIM, and DINO scores. GPT-5 provides moderate reconstruction quality but with notably lower success rates. Furthermore, OmniLottie produces substantially richer token sequences than all baselines, enabling more expressive and detailed vector animation generation. 5.3. Qualitative Evaluation The qualitative comparisons in Figs. 4 to 6 show that OmniLottie delivers markedly better results across Text-toLottie, Text-Image-to-Lottie, and Video-to-Lottie tasks. For Text-to-Lottie, OmniLottie achieves strong prompt alignment and high visual fidelity. In contrast, the commercial tool Recraft though capable of producing complex vector graphicsexhibits repetitive, poorly aligned motion, while GPT-5 suffers from high failure rates and low-quality Lottie outputs even when successful. For Text-Image-to-Lottie, VLM-based approaches such as GPT-5 frequently fail and show weak alignment to both the reference image and text prompt. Optimization-based methods (AniClipart, LiveSketch) require long processing times, depend on raster-to-vector conversion before animation, cannot output native vector formats, and yield suboptimal visual results. OmniLottie generates true vector animations with much shorter latency, high success rates, and superior visual quality. For Video-to-Lottie, Qwen2.5-VL and Gemini fail Figure 4. Qualitative comparison of Text-to-Lottie generation. Methods that failed to generate valid animations are omitted to ensure clear comparison. to produce valid outputs, and GPT-5 yields occasional but low-quality conversions. In contrast, OmniLottie produces high-quality vector animations that capture both motion patterns and visual characteristics from the input videos. 5.4. Ablation Studies Impact of SVG Data. We evaluate different LottieSVG mixing ratios in ablation studies (Tab. 2, Fig. 7), including pure Lottie, Lottie with high SVG proportion, pure SVG, and Lottie with moderate SVG proportion (our final choice). Results show that moderate mixing yields the best performance on Text-to-Lottie and TextImage-to-Lottie tasks. Lottie provides complex geometry and motion, while SVG contributes diverse shapes with simpler motion. Co-training improves geometric understanding, but excessive SVG biases the model toward simplistic motion, reducing Motion Alignment scores. Moderate mixing balances geometric richness with motion complexity, achieving optimal overall performance. Necessity of the Lottie Tokenizer. We evaluate the impact of our Lottie Tokenizer via ablations on Text-to-, Figure 5. Qualitative comparison of Text-Image-to-Lottie generation. Methods that failed to generate valid animations are omitted to ensure clear comparison. Text-Image-to-, and Video-to-Lottie tasks. Three configurations of Qwen2.5-VL are compared: pretrained, fine-tuned on raw JSON, and fine-tuned with our tokenizer. Metrics include FVD, CLIP, object and motion alignment for text/image tasks, and FVD, PSNR, SSIM, DINO for video. As shown in Tab. 3 and Fig. 8, our tokenizer consistently improves performance across all tasks and metrics, demonstrating that its structured, compact representation enhances generation quality and computational efficiency. 6. Conclusions and Limitations Conclusion. We introduce OmniLottie, unified framework for multi-modal vector animation generation. Leveraging our large-scale MMLottie-2M dataset, OmniLottie handles Text-, Text-Image-, and Video-to-Lottie tasks. Combined with the Lottie tokenizer and pretrained VLM, it efficiently processes multi-modal inputs using compact token sequences. Experiments show that OmniLottie produces high-quality animations with strong visual fidelity and semantic alignment. Figure 6. Qualitative comparison of Video-to-Lottie generation.
